title: Pod starting with Quadlet before my NFS mount point mounted
body:
### Issue Description

Hello,

As explained [here ](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23273), I have a Pod running with Quadlet, this pod use a Volume which is a NFS mount point, but at boot, podman seems to start before rpcbind, thus, my directory is empty inside my container.

I tried to set this kind of options in my .kube file but none work :

[Unit]
Description=My instance
After=network-online.target remote-fs.target
Wants=network-online.target remote-fs.target


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Additional environment details

Redhat9

comments.body:
Hello, and thanks for the issue.  If you are in fact on a RH product, I would recommend you report this issue via those channels.  It makes things easier for us and you.

That said, I wonder if @ygalblum might have recommendation?

@Jean-PhilippeD I assume your `.kube` Quadlet is a user service right? If so, since `remote-fs.target` is a root service, your service cannot depend on it.

We had a similar issue with waiting on network. To address that, podman brings a user level unit that waits on the root network one:
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/32d6c540542df8bf2115a8600fd4fbbf4b6b837a/contrib/systemd/user/podman-user-wait-network-online.service

Quadlet uses it here: 
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/32d6c540542df8bf2115a8600fd4fbbf4b6b837a/pkg/systemd/quadlet/quadlet.go#L2221

So, you can do the same by creating your own user level service (replacing `network-online.target` with `remote-fs.target`) and having your service depend on it

yes my quadlet is a user service, I was not sure if I could rely on a root service.
Thanks for explanations, I gonna try your solution !
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

just wanted to add to this for anyone coming across this in future. `Wants=` may not be strong enough of a dependency. For reasons which are beyond me, systemd will still start a service even when its `Wants=` service fails with timeout.

For eg. as a very basic test

```
cat wait.service
[Service]
Type=oneshot
TimeoutStartSec=20s
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c 'until false; do sleep 0.5; done'
RemainAfterExit=yes

cat service.service
[Unit]
Wants=wait.service
After=wait.service

[Service]
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c 'sleep 20'
```

```
Jan 12 19:47:11 thomas-t15p2 systemd[2920]: Starting wait.service...
Job for wait.service failed because a timeout was exceeded.
See "systemctl --user status wait.service" and "journalctl --user -xeu wait.service" for details.
Jan 12 19:47:31 thomas-t15p2 systemd[2920]: wait.service: start operation timed out. Terminating.
Jan 12 19:47:31 thomas-t15p2 systemd[2920]: wait.service: Main process exited, code=killed, status=15/TERM
Jan 12 19:47:31 thomas-t15p2 systemd[2920]: wait.service: Failed with result 'timeout'.
Jan 12 19:47:31 thomas-t15p2 systemd[2920]: Failed to start wait.service.
Jan 12 19:47:31 thomas-t15p2 systemd[2920]: Started service.service.
```

> [@Jean-PhilippeD](https://github.com/Jean-PhilippeD) BTW did you get to try my suggestion?

Yes I did that and it's working. Thanks, sorry for the late reply..
